driver.body: Density without safety is a liability that regulators close overnight. This driver owns mobility's single contribution to **Trust (T)** — the one moat term besides Density (D) that mobility meaningfully moves — and its logic is narrow but load-bearing: accumulated safe miles are not an asset until they become a **disclosed, auditable record** that regulators and riders both price in [S-011]. Miles that stay inside Kepler's own warehouse move nothing; miles turned into an inspectable safety record before the next zone permit are what actually lift T. The two mechanisms below are the currency and the ledger — the metric that proves the fleet is getting safer, and the timeline that lets an outside party verify it.

mechanisms.body:
The operating metric is **miles between safety-critical disengagements** — the distance the fleet covers before a remote operator or safety driver has to take control to avoid harm [S-012]. Density compounds this metric in a way a single-vertical rival cannot copy: saturated zones run so many miles over the same road geometry that the model sees each rare edge-case — an occluded pedestrian stepping from behind a bus, a cyclist jaywalking against the light — hundreds of times rather than once, so the long tail is learned rather than survived. The mechanism moves T by pushing miles-per-intervention up an order of magnitude, and it is fed by D6's perception models, which are themselves trained on the density D1 produces [S-105] — the cross-feed that makes the gain circular rather than one-off.

The evidence is superlinear, and the superlinearity is the whole claim: miles-per-intervention rose **11.6× across four quarters while fleet miles merely tripled** [S-106]. If safety scaled linearly with exposure the two curves would track each other; instead the ratio pulls away, and that widening gap is the density dividend — the same edge-case seen enough times to be modelled instead of merely endured. The trade-off is real and is managed, not ignored: chasing the metric too hard breeds over-cautious "freezing" behaviour that tanks the rider experience of Driver 3, so the safety and experience teams are held to one shared objective function, not two [S-012].

Trust is not the average day; it is **how the worst days are handled and disclosed**. Kepler maintains a public safety-incident timeline — every safety-critical event logged, root-caused, and tied to the exact model or hardware change that closed it [S-013]. The mechanism moves T by converting opaque risk into an audit trail a regulator can inspect before granting the next zone permit [S-107]: disclosure itself becomes the currency that buys licence-to-operate. The trade-off is radical transparency — publishing incidents competitors keep quiet, a genuine short-term reputational cost paid deliberately in exchange for long-term standing with the bodies that hand out permits.

The Q4 event is the instructive edge-case. Heavy rain attenuated lidar; rather than proceeding blind, the vehicle correctly executed a **minimum-risk pullover**, and the fix — radar-weighted sensor fusion — came straight from Devices (D5) hardware co-design [S-109]. That is the flywheel made visible inside a single incident: a hardware cross-feed closing a mobility safety gap, then logged in the open where a regulator can trace cause to remedy. The chain to Driver 3 follows directly — a fleet that is both dense and demonstrably safe can finally be sold as an experience, because trust is the precondition a rider needs before any of the app's promises mean anything [S-013].
